petitioner_background: The beneficiary is employed as an executive director of clinical research, and the petitioner is a healthcare provider. The petitioner sought classification for the beneficiary as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. The beneficiary was claimed to have created five Accountable Care Organizations that reduced unnecessary testing and returned savings to Medicare funds, and was credited with obtaining FDA approvals for five drugs. The beneficiary was in H-1B nonimmigrant status at the time of filing.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The decision does not detail the specific membership evidence submitted on motion; the AAO's prior October 9/19, 2013 decision had addressed this criterion and found the petitioner did not meet it.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO's prior decision concluded that the beneficiary did not meet the membership criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(ii). The motion to reconsider did not specifically challenge this determination with pertinent legal authority or allegations of error.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The decision does not specify the particular judging evidence, but the AAO's prior final decision had determined the beneficiary met this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO's prior decision determined that the beneficiary met the judging criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iv). This was the only criterion of the ten regulatory criteria that the beneficiary was found to have satisfied.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that the beneficiary created five Accountable Care Organizations that each and cumulatively reduced unnecessary testing and allowed savings to be put back into the Medicare fund. The petitioner also claimed the beneficiary was acknowledged for obtaining FDA approvals for five drugs.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO's prior decision discussed the petitioner's documentary evidence, including the claims regarding savings to Medicare funding and FDA approvals, as they related to this criterion, and determined the petitioner had not established eligibility under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(v). On motion, the petitioner did not specifically allege how the AAO erred in that analysis or cite pertinent legal authority to support reconsideration.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The decision does not detail the specific leading or critical role evidence; the prior AAO decision addressed and rejected this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO's prior decision determined the beneficiary did not meet the leading or critical role criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii). The motion to reconsider did not raise specific allegations of error regarding this determination or cite pertinent legal authority.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner did not contest the director's findings on appeal. On motion, the petitioner argued that by virtue of being on H-1B status, the beneficiary is restricted from receiving a high salary or other significantly high remuneration.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner abandoned this criterion on appeal by not contesting the director's findings regarding 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ix). The AAO cited Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen. and Hristov v. Roark for the proposition that claims not raised on appeal are deemed abandoned. The AAO rejected the H-1B salary restriction argument, noting the petitioner cited no legal authority to support the conclusion that alleged salary restrictions as a nonimmigrant demonstrate eligibility, which requires evidence meeting three of the ten criteria.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner abandoned this criterion on appeal; no specific evidence was discussed on motion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner abandoned the published material criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii) on appeal. The AAO noted that claims not raised on appeal are deemed abandoned, citing Sepulveda and Hristov.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner abandoned this criterion on appeal; no specific evidence was discussed on motion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner abandoned the scholarly articles criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi) on appeal. The AAO noted that claims not raised on appeal are deemed abandoned.

outcome_reasoning: The motion to reconsider was dismissed because it failed to meet procedural and substantive requirements. Procedurally, the petitioner did not submit the required statement regarding whether the validity of the decision is the subject of any judicial proceeding, as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii). Substantively, the motion did not allege application of precedent to a novel situation, new precedent, or a change in law, nor did it specify factual or legal issues decided in error or overlooked. The petitioner's argument that USCIS failed to properly estimate the total population of the field was rejected because the beneficiary must first meet the antecedent procedural requirement of submitting qualifying evidence under three of the ten criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) before USCIS considers whether the beneficiary is at the very top of the field, per Kazarian. The previous AAO decision was affirmed and the petition remained denied.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The motion to reconsider failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii) because the petitioner did not submit the required statement regarding whether the validity of the unfavorable decision has been or is the subject of any judicial proceeding. Under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(4), a motion that does not meet applicable requirements shall be dismissed. Additionally, the motion failed to state specific allegations of factual or legal error or cite pertinent precedent, and the petitioner was found to have abandoned the published material, scholarly articles, and high salary criteria by not contesting them on appeal.
